Projected Gauss-Seidel

On this page

  • The core problem

  • Reducing to the constraint multiplier space

  • Extending to contacts

  • The block diagonal preconditioner

  • The PGS algorithm

  • Convergence check

  • Difference from Projected Jacobi

  • Config keys

The core problem

The Moreau time-stepping scheme produces the scaled linear system used by the solvers. In compact form:

\[\mathcal{S}\,\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{b}_n\]

See the Moreau chapter Time-Stepping for Non-Smooth Mechanical Systems for the full derivation and the detailed block definitions of \(\mathcal{S}\) and \(\mathbf{b}_n\).

Reducing to the constraint multiplier space

Eliminating \(u_{n+1}\) from the second and third block rows via the Schur complement gives a reduced system purely in the constraint multipliers \(\hat\Lambda = (\Lambda_g^\top, \Lambda_\gamma^\top)^\top\):

\[\mathbf{S}\,\hat\Lambda = \tilde{b},\]

where the Schur complement matrix is:

\[\mathbf{S} = \hat{W}^\top M^{-1} \hat{W} - \hat{C},\]

with \(\hat{W} = (W_g, W_\gamma)\) and block-diagonal \(\hat{C} = \mathrm{diag}(-C/(\theta\,dt^2),\; -A/(\theta\,dt))\). The right-hand side is:

\[\tilde{b} = \hat{W}^\top M^{-1} b_u - \hat{b}\]

where \(b_u\) and \(\hat{b}\) are the corresponding blocks of the full right-hand side. Once \(\hat\Lambda\) is known, the velocity is recovered as:

\[u_{n+1} = \underbrace{M^{-1} b_u}_{u_\mathrm{free}} - \underbrace{M^{-1} \hat{W}\,\hat\Lambda}_{u_\mathrm{corr}}\]

The matrix-vector product \(\mathbf{S}\hat\Lambda\) is evaluated through the velocity correction:

\[u_\mathrm{corr} = M^{-1}\hat{W}\hat\Lambda, \qquad \mathbf{S}\hat\Lambda = \hat{W}^\top u_\mathrm{corr} - \hat{C}\hat\Lambda.\]

This avoids ever forming \(\mathbf{S}\) explicitly.

Extending to contacts

Adding contacts appends the contact Jacobian \(W_{NT}\) as additional rows. The extended system becomes:

\[\begin{split}\begin{pmatrix} M & \hat{W} & W_{NT} \\ \hat{W}^\top & -\hat{C} & 0 \\ W_{NT}^\top & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} u_{n+1} \\ \hat\Lambda \\ \Lambda_{NT} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} b_u \\ \hat{b} \\ b_{NT} \end{pmatrix}\end{split}\]

The zero block in the lower right means contact rows have no compliance term. Contact impulses are not determined by this linear system alone; they are subject to the non-smooth contact projection (Signorini + Coulomb).

The full multiplier vector in the implementation is therefore:

\[\begin{split}\begin{pmatrix} \Lambda_g \\ \Lambda_\gamma \\ \Lambda_{NT} \end{pmatrix}\end{split}\]

The block diagonal preconditioner

PGS preconditions each block \(j\) with the diagonal of the Schur complement restricted to that block:

\[D_j = \hat{W}_j^\top M^{-1} \hat{W}_j - \hat{C}_j\]

For constraint patterns (hinges, beams, etc.) this is a small dense matrix (up to 6x6) that may be inverted implicitly using a Cholesky factorization.

The PGS algorithm

PGS differs from Projected Jacobi in that it updates each block sequentially and propagates the velocity correction \(u_\mathrm{corr}\) immediately, so later blocks in the same sweep already see the effect of earlier updates.

Algorithm: Projected Gauss-Seidel for Contact Dynamics
-------------------------------------------------------
Input : rhs, W, M^{-1}, D^{-1}, k_max, relaxation
Output: u_{n+1}

1. Initialize λ ← λ_warm_start
2. u_corr ← M^{-1} W^T λ

3. for k = 0 to k_max do
4.     for each block j in order do
5.         r_j ← rhs_j - W_j u_corr - C_j λ_j
6.         Δλ_j ← ω · D_j^{-1} · r_j
7.         if j is contact block then Δλ_j ← α · Δλ_j

8.         λ_j ← λ_j + Δλ_j

9.         if j is frictionless then
10.            λ_j ← min(λ_j, 0)
11.        else if j is frictional then
12.            λ_j ← ProjectToCoulombDisk(λ_j)

13.        Δλ_j_proj ← λ_j - (λ_j_old)
14.        u_corr ← u_corr + M^{-1} W_j^T Δλ_j_proj

15.    check convergence (||Δλ|| < ε)

16. u_{n+1} ← u_free - u_corr
17. store λ and warm-start data for next step

Note

The implementation stores normal contact impulses with a negative sign convention inherited from the way the contact Jacobian is assembled.

Convergence check

The same mixed absolute/relative residual is used as for Projected Jacobi:

\[\mathrm{res}^{(k)} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n_u}} \left\lVert \frac{u^{(k+1)} - u^{(k)}} {\varepsilon_\mathrm{abs}\,\mathbf{1} + \varepsilon_\mathrm{rel}\,\max(|u^{(k+1)}|,|u^{(k)}|)} \right\rVert_2 \leq 1\]

Nesterov acceleration and warmstart are supported with the same behaviour as Projected Jacobi (see Projected Jacobi).

Difference from Projected Jacobi

Both solvers iterate on contact impulses and use the same preconditioner diagonal, but:

  • PJ updates all blocks using the velocity from the previous iteration and only then propagates the combined impulse change. This makes each sweep embarrassingly parallel.

  • PGS propagates each block’s impulse change to \(u_\mathrm{corr}\) immediately before moving to the next block. Later blocks therefore use more up-to-date information within the same sweep.

In practice PGS hopes to achieve better asymptotic runtime than solving the full constraint matrix using LU decomposition.

Config keys

Key

Effect

solver.max_iterations

Hard iteration cap (default 200000)

solver.tol_abs, solver.tol_rel

Convergence tolerances on the velocity residual

solver.relaxation

Over-relaxation factor on all solver updates

solver.alpha

Step-size multiplier for only contact impulse updates

solver.nesterov

Enable Nesterov momentum (default false)

solver.warmstart

Reuse previous-step impulses as initial guess (default true)
